Here are three moves in breaking, the newest Olympic sport, that appear to defy the laws of physics.

The 2024 Olympics in Paris debuted a new sport: breaking (also known as breakdancing, but don't call it that). Here are three moves that appear to defy the laws of physics, and how they work.

01/17/2025

NOVAis available to stream on pbs.org and the free PBS App, available on iPhone, Apple TV, Android TV, Android smartphones, Amazon Fire TV, Amazon Fire Tablet, Roku, Samsung Smart TV, and Vizio.

- The 2024 Olympics in Paris is debuting a new sport, breaking, also known as break dancing.

But don't call it that.

Breaking is a battle style of dance that started in the 70s in the Bronx, New York, where breakers freestyle acrobatics and complex footwork to a beat.

Here are three kinds of moves that appear to defy the laws of physics.

Spins are power moves in a breaker's arsenal, where they balance on either their hands, even their heads.

The magic in this move is how the breaker generates and controls their speed with their legs.

Kind of like how a figure skater speeds up when they move their arms in closer.

In physics, this is called conservation of angular momentum.

Floats are another kind of power move that has a ton of variations.

For this move, the breaker uses their arms to seemingly float off the floor.

This requires a ton of core strength.

You thought planks were hard?

And a good sense of knowing where their center of gravity is, or where their weight is evenly distributed.

And then there are freezes, which is when a breaker holds a move or shape with their body for a few seconds.

It's usually used to hit a beat in the music or to signify the end of the sequence.

As you can imagine, holding a pose like this one requires a lot of balance.

This is called static equilibrium, meaning that all the forces on the front, back, left, and right are equal.

See how many of these moves you can catch when you watch the Olympics.

(soft upbeat music)
